name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ ۖ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ ٱلدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ ۖ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِى وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِى لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ (١٨٦)
```

```english
When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me. So let them respond to Me, and let them have faith in Me, so that they may fare rightly. (186)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** "When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me. So let them respond to Me, and let them believe in Me, so that they may be rightly guided." (2:186). This verse is the finest expression of its intended meaning and possesses the most delicate and beautiful style. Its foundation is built upon the first-person singular speaker ("I", "Me") rather than the third-person ("He") or other forms, which indicates a perfection of concern (العناية) for the matter. Furthermore, His saying, "My servants" (عبادي), rather than "the people" or similar terms, increases this concern. Then, He omitted the intermediary in the response, saying: "I am indeed nearmost" (فإني قريب), rather than saying: "Tell them that I am nearmost." This is followed by the emphasis with "indeed" (إنّ), and the use of the adjective (قريب) rather than a verb to denote nearness, which signifies the stability and permanence of this proximity. Then, He indicates the renewal and continuity of the response by using the present-tense verb (أجيب) which denotes both. Finally, He conditions the response—meaning His saying: "I answer the supplicant’s call"—with His saying: "when he calls Me" (إذا دعان). This condition adds nothing to the phrase "the supplicant’s call" (دعوة الداع) except to emphasize its very essence. This indicates that the call of the supplicant is answered without any [external] condition or restriction, similar to His saying, the Exalted: "Call Me, I will answer you" (40:60).

These are seven subtle points in the verse that signal the great importance and concern given to the answering of supplication (الدعاء). Moreover, despite its brevity, the first-person pronoun is repeated seven times in the verse—a unique characteristic not found in any other verse of the Qur'an.

Supplication (الدعاء) and calling (الدعوة) consist of directing the attention of the one called toward the caller. Asking (السؤال) is the seeking of a benefit or the drawing of a gift from the one asked to fulfill the need of the asker after directing his attention. Thus, asking is like the goal of supplication, and it is the comprehensive meaning for all instances of asking, such as asking to remove ignorance, asking in the sense of accounting, asking in the sense of seeking sustenance, and so on.

Furthermore, servitude (العبودية) — *the state of being a slave or servant* — as previously mentioned, is the state of being owned (المملوكية). However, it is not just any ownership, but specifically the ownership of a human being. A servant (العبد) is a human, or any being possessed of intellect and perception, such as the angels when attributed to Him, the Exalted.

His ownership (الملك), the Exalted, differs from the ownership of others as reality differs from a mere claim, or as the literal differs from the metaphorical. For He, the Exalted, owns His servants with an absolute ownership that encompasses them; they possess no independence apart from Him, neither in their essences nor in what follows their essences of attributes and actions, nor in anything else attributed to them such as spouses, children, wealth, or status. Everything they own by virtue of its attribution to them in some way—as in our saying: "his self," "his body," "his hearing," "his sight," "his action," and "his trace," which are types of natural and real ownership (الملك بالطبع والحقيقة); or our saying: "his spouse," "his wealth," "his status," and "his right," which are types of conventional and relative ownership (الملك بالوضع والاعتبار)—they only own by His permission, the Exalted, in establishing the relationship between them and what they own, whatever it may be, and by His granting of ownership. Thus, Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), is the One who attributed their souls and essences to them; had He not willed it, He would not have attributed them, and they would not have existed at all. He is the One who made for them hearing, sight, and hearts, and He is the One who created everything and ordained it with a precise ordainment.

Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Intervener between a thing and itself, and He is the Intervener between a thing and all that accompanies it: child, spouse, friend, wealth, status, or right. He is closer to His creation than any assumed thing. He, the Glorified, is nearmost in an absolute sense, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنكُمْ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تُبْصِرُونَ** — "and We are nearer to him than you are, though you do not perceive" (56:85). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ** — "and We are nearer to him than [his] jugular vein" (50:16). And He, the Exalted, said: **أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ** — "and know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart" (8:24); and the heart (القلب) is the perceiving soul (النفس المدركة).

In short, His real ownership of His servants and their being servants to Him is what necessitates His being absolutely near to them, and closer to them than anything else when compared. This ownership, which necessitates the permissibility of every disposal He wills, however He wills, without any repeller or preventer, dictates that it is for Allah, the Glorified, to answer any supplication made by any of His creation and to fulfill the need they asked for through His giving and disposal. For His ownership is universal, and His authority and encompassment are established in all circumstances without being restricted to one state over another. This is not as the Jews say: that when Allah created things and ordained the measures, the matter was concluded, and the reins of new disposal left His hand due to the decree He made mandatory—thus there is no abrogation (نسخ), no change in divine will (بداء), and no answering of supplication because the matter is already finished. Nor is it as a group from this community says: that Allah has no hand in the actions of His servants; these are the *Qadariyya*, whom the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم called the "Magians of this community," as narrated by both schools in his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: **القدرية مجوس هذه الامة** — "The *Qadariyya* are the Magians of this community."

Rather, the kingdom belongs to Allah, the Glorified, absolutely. Nothing owns anything except by His granting of ownership and His permission. Whatever He wills, owns, and permits to occur, occurs; and whatever He does not will, does not own, and does not permit, does not occur, even if every effort and care were exerted toward its occurrence. He, the Exalted, said: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ** — "O mankind! It is you who need Allah, and it is Allah who is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable" (35:15).

It has thus become clear that His saying, the Exalted: "When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me," just as it contains the ruling—namely, the answering of supplication—it also contains its causes. The fact that the supplicants are servants of Allah is what necessitates His nearness to them, and His nearness to them is what necessitates His absolute answering of their supplication. The absolute nature of the response entails the absolute nature of the supplication; thus, every supplication made, He answers. However, there is a point here: He, the Exalted, conditioned His saying, "I answer the supplicant’s call," with His saying, "when he calls Me" (إذا دعان). This condition, which adds nothing to the subject itself, indicates the requirement of reality (الحقيقة) rather than metaphor (التجوز) or semblance (الشبه). For our saying: "Listen to the advice of the counselor when he counsels you," or "Honor the scholar if he is a scholar," indicates the necessity of him being characterized by what the reality requires. Thus, the counselor who intends counsel by his speech is the one whose speech must be listened to, and the scholar who is realized in his knowledge and acts upon what he knows is the one who must be honored.

Therefore, His saying, "when he calls Me," indicates that the promise of an absolute response is only when the caller is a caller in reality—intending [the request] according to his primordial (الفطري) and instinctive knowledge, with his tongue being in harmony with his heart. For the reality of supplication and asking is that which the heart carries and which the tongue of the primordial nature (لسان الفطرة) calls out with, not that which is produced by the tongue that moves however it is turned—whether truthfully or falsely, seriously or jokingly, literally or metaphorically. For this reason, you see that He, the Exalted, counted as "asking" that in which the tongue has no part. He, the Exalted, said: **وَآتَاكُم مِّن كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِن تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَا إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ** — "And He gave you all that you had asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed man is very unjust and very ungrateful" (14:34). In the blessings they cannot count, they are callers and askers, yet they did not ask for them with their outward tongues, but rather with the tongue of their need and entitlement—a primordial, existential tongue. He, the Exalted, also said: **يَسْأَلُهُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ** — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is engaged in some work" (55:29). Its indication of what we have mentioned is even more manifest and clear.

Thus, the primordial asking (السؤال الفطري) from Allah, the Glorified, never misses the response. As for the supplication that is not answered and does not meet with a response, it lacks one of two things, both of which He mentioned in His saying: "the supplicant’s call (دعوة الداع) when he calls Me (إذا دعان)."

Either a [real] supplication has not been realized there, and the matter was merely confused for the caller—such as a person calling and asking for what cannot be, while being ignorant of that, or asking for what he would not want if the reality of the matter were disclosed to him. For example, he might call and ask for the healing of a sick person, not the reviving of the dead; had he been empowered and called for his life, as the Prophets used to ask, his life would have been restored, but he is in despair of that. Or he asks for something which, if he knew its reality, he would not ask for; thus, he is not answered in it.

Or, the asking is realized, but not from Allah alone—such as one who asks Allah for one of his needs while his heart is attached to conventional causes (الاسباب العادية) or to illusory matters he imagines to be sufficient for his affair or influential in his state. Thus, he has not made the supplication sincere for Allah, the Glorified, and has not asked Allah in reality. For Allah, who answers supplications, is the One who has no partner in His affair, not the one who works through the partnership of causes and illusions. These two groups of callers and askers have not made the supplication sincere with the heart, even if they made it sincere with their tongues.

This is the summary of the discourse on supplication as provided by the verse. Through this, the meanings of other verses revealed on this subject become clear, such as His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَا يَعْبَأُ بِكُمْ رَبِّي لَوْلَا دُعَاؤُكُمْ** — "Say, ‘My Lord would not care for you were it not for your supplication’" (25:77); and His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَاكُمْ عَذَابُ اللَّهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ السَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ تَدْعُونَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ مَا تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِن شَاءَ وَتَنسَوْنَ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ** — "Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment come to you, or should the Hour come to you, will you call upon anyone other than Allah, should you be truthful?’ No, Him you will call, and He will remove that for which you called Him, if He wishes, and you will forget what you used to ascribe [to Him] as partners" (6:40-41); and His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَن يُنَجِّيكُم مِّن ظُلُمَاتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً لَّئِنْ أَنجَانَا مِنْ هَٰذِهِ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ قُلِ اللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُم مِّنْهَا وَمِن كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ** — "Say, ‘Who delivers you from the darkness of land and sea, [when] you call Him out of humility and in secret: ‘‘If He delivers us from this, we will surely be among the grateful’’?’ Say, ‘It is Allah who delivers you from them and from every distress, yet you ascribe partners [to Him]’" (6:63-64).

These verses indicate that man possesses an instinctive supplication and a primordial asking with which he asks his Lord. However, when he is in ease and prosperity, his soul becomes attached to causes and associates them with his Lord. Thus, the matter becomes confused for him, and he imagines that he does not call his Lord nor ask of Him, even though he asks none other than Him—for he is upon the primordial nature (الفطرة), and there is no change in the creation of Allah, the Exalted. When hardship occurs and the causes lose their influence, and the partners and intercessors are lost, it becomes clear to him that none can fulfill his need or answer his request except Allah. He then returns to his primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري), forgets every cause among the causes, and turns his face toward the Generous Lord. He then removes his hardship, fulfills his need, and shades him with ease. Then, when he becomes entangled in it again, he returns to what he was upon before of polytheism and forgetfulness.

Similarly, His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَستَجِبْ لَكُمْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ** — "Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased" (40:60). This verse invites to supplication and promises a response, and it goes further by naming supplication "worship" (العبادة) in its saying: "to worship Me" (عن عبادتي), meaning "to call upon Me." In fact, it makes worship in its entirety a form of supplication, as it includes a threat of Fire for abandoning supplication. The threat of Fire is only for abandoning worship altogether, not for abandoning some of its types to the exclusion of others. Thus, the essence of worship is supplication; so understand that.

Through this, the meaning of other verses in this chapter becomes clear, such as His saying, the Exalted: **فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** — "So call Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him" (40:14); and His saying, the Exalted: **وَادْعُوهُ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِّنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "and call Him with fear and hope. Indeed Allah’s mercy is close to the virtuous" (7:56); and His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَدْعُونَنَا رَغَبًا وَرَهَبًا وَكَانُوا لَنَا خَاشِعِينَ** — "and they would call Us with hope and fear, and they were humble before Us" (21:90); and His saying, the Exalted: **ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً إِنَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ** — "Supplicate to your Lord, humbly and secretly. Indeed He does not like the transgressors" (7:55); and His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ نِدَاءً خَفِيًّا** — "when he called out to his Lord with a secret cry" (19:3), until His saying: **وَلَمْ أَكُن بِدُعَائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا** — "and I have never been disappointed in supplicating You, my Lord" (19:4); and His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِ** — "And He answers those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and He enhances them out of His grace" (42:26), and other similar verses. These verses contain the pillars of supplication and the etiquette of the caller. The most essential of these is sincerity (الإخلاص) in supplicating Him, the Exalted, which is the harmony of the heart with the tongue, the cutting off from every cause besides Allah, and the attachment to Him, the Exalted. To this are joined fear, hope, desire, dread, humility, entreaty, persistence, remembrance, righteous deeds, faith, the etiquette of presence, and other things contained in the narrations.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "So let them respond to Me, and let them believe in Me," is a derivation from what the preceding sentence indicates by implication: that Allah, the Exalted, is near to His servants, nothing intervenes between Him and their supplication, and He is possessed of concern for them and for what they ask of Him. Thus, He invites them to supplicate Him, being characterized by this attribute. So let them respond to Him in this invitation, let them turn toward Him, and let them believe in Him in this description, and let them be certain that He is nearmost and answering, "so that they may be rightly guided" in their supplication to Him.

## (Narrative Discussion)

From the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, as narrated by both schools: **الدعاء سلاح المؤمن** — "Supplication is the weapon of the believer." And in *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*, in a *hadith qudsi*: "O Moses, ask Me for everything you need, even the fodder for your sheep and the salt for your dough."

In *al-Makarim*, from him (peace be upon him): "Supplication is better than reciting the Qur'an, because Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **قُلْ مَا يَعْبَأُ بِكُمْ رَبِّي لَوْلَا دُعَاؤُكُمْ** (Say, ‘My Lord would not care for you were it not for your supplication’)." This has also been narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon them).

In *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*, in a narration by Muhammad ibn ‘Ajlan from Muhammad ibn ‘Ubaydillah ibn ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn, from his cousin al-Sadiq, from his fathers, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, who said: "Allah revealed to one of His prophets in some of His revelation: ‘By My Might and My Majesty, I shall surely cut off the hope of every hoper who hopes in other than Me with despair, and I shall surely clothe him in the garment of humiliation among the people, and I shall surely distance him from My relief and My grace. Does My servant hope in other than Me during hardships, while the hardships are in My hand? And does he hope in someone besides Me, while I am the All-sufficient, the All-generous? In My hand are the keys to the doors, and they are closed, while My door is open to whoever calls Me?’" (the hadith continues).

Also in *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, who said: "Allah said: ‘There is no creature who seeks refuge in a creature besides Me except that I cut off the means of the heavens and the means of the earth from him; then if he asks Me, I do not give to him, and if he calls Me, I do not answer him. And there is no creature who seeks refuge in Me besides My creation except that I guarantee the heavens and the earth for his sustenance; then if he calls Me, I answer him, if he asks Me, I give to him, and if he seeks My forgiveness, I forgive him.’"

**I say:** What these two hadiths contain is sincerity (الإخلاص) in supplication. It is not a nullification of the causality of existential causes (الاسباب الوجودية) which Allah, the Exalted, has made as intermediary means between things and their existential needs, not as independent, overflowing causes besides Allah, the Glorified. Man has an inner perception of this, for he perceives by his primordial nature that for his need there is a cause that grants it and whose action does not fail. He also perceives that every outward cause he turns to can fail in its effect. Thus, he perceives that the Principle from which every matter begins, and the Pillar upon which every need relies and leans for its realization and existence, is something other than these causes. The necessary consequence of this is that he should not rely with complete reliance on any of these causes such that he cuts himself off from the True Cause and seeks refuge in that outward cause. Man moves toward this reality with the slightest attention and reflection. Thus, when he asks or seeks something of his needs and what he sought occurs, it reveals that he asked his Lord and that his need—which he perceived through his inner perception—connected through the path of causes to his Lord, and he received grace from Him. If he seeks that from one of the causes [alone], it is not from an inner primordial perception, but rather a matter depicted for him by his imagination due to factors that necessitated this imagination, without an inner perception of the need. This is one of the instances where the inner (الباطن) contradicts the outward (الظاهر).

A parallel to this is that man often loves something and is concerned with it, until when it occurs, he finds it harmful to what is more beneficial, more important, and more beloved to him; so he leaves the first and takes the second. Sometimes he flees from something, until when he encounters it, he finds it more beneficial and better than what he was guarding himself with; so he takes the first and leaves the second. A sick child, when offered bitter medicine, refuses to drink it and begins to cry, yet he wants health. Thus, by his inner primordial perception, he is asking for health and therefore asking for the medicine, even if by the tongue of his speech or action he is asking for the opposite. Thus, man has a system in his life according to primordial understanding and inner perception, and he has another system according to his imagination. In the primordial system, no error occurs, nor is there any confusion in its course. As for the imaginary system, error and oversight often occur in it. Thus, man might ask or seek something according to the imaginary form, while by this very asking, he is asking for something else or its opposite. Upon this, the meaning of the hadiths should be established, and it is what is apparent from the saying of ‘Ali (peace be upon him) that will follow: "The gift is in proportion to the intention" (the hadith continues).

In *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Supplicate to Allah while you are certain of the response."

In a *hadith qudsi*: "I am as My servant expects Me to be, so let him not expect of Me anything but good."

**I say:** This is because supplication accompanied by despair or hesitation reveals a lack of asking in reality, as previously mentioned. Indeed, supplication for what cannot be has been prohibited.

Also in *al-‘Udda*, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Flee to Allah in your needs, and seek refuge in Him in your calamities, and entreat Him and call Him, for supplication is the marrow of worship. No believer calls Allah except that He answers: either He hastens it for him in this world, or He delays it for him in the Hereafter, or He expiates his sins in proportion to his supplication, as long as he does not supplicate for a sin."

In *Nahj al-Balagha*, in his (peace be upon him) testament to his son al-Husayn (peace be upon him): "Then He placed in your hands the keys to His treasuries by what He permitted you of asking Him. So whenever you wish, you may open the doors of His blessings through supplication and seek the downpour of His mercy. Let not the delay in His response cause you to despair, for the gift is in proportion to the intention. Sometimes the response is delayed for you so that it may be a greater reward for the asker and a more bountiful gift for the hoper. Sometimes you ask for a thing and you are not given it, but you are given something better than it, sooner or later, or it is diverted from you for what is better for you. For perhaps a matter you have sought contains the destruction of your religion were you to be given it. So let your asking be for that whose beauty remains for you and whose burden is removed from you; for wealth does not remain for you, nor do you remain for it."

**I say:** His saying: "for the gift is in proportion to the intention," he (peace be upon him) means by it that the response corresponds to the call. What the asker asks of Him, the Exalted, according to what his true conscience is set upon and what the depth of his heart carries, is what he is given—not what his speech discloses and his words manifest. For the word sometimes does not correspond to the sought meaning in every respect, as previously explained. This is the finest sentence and most comprehensive word for explaining the connection between the asking and the response.

He (peace be upon him) has explained through it several instances where the response seemingly fails to meet the call outwardly, such as the delay in the response, or the replacement of the thing asked for in this world with what is better than it in this world, or with what is better than it in the Hereafter, or its diversion to something else more suitable for the state of the asker. For the asker might ask for a pleasant blessing, but if it were given to him immediately, it would not be pleasant or according to his desire; thus its response is delayed because the asker, by asking for a pleasant blessing, has in reality asked for a delayed response. Similarly, the believer concerned with the matter of his religion, if he asks for what contains the destruction of his religion while he does not know it and imagines that his happiness lies in it—whereas his happiness is in his Hereafter—he has in reality asked for his Hereafter, not his world; thus he is answered in it, not in this world.

In *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "No servant spreads his hand toward Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, except that Allah is ashamed to return it empty, until He places in it of His grace and mercy what He wills. So when one of you supplicates, let him not return his hand until he wipes it over his head and face." In another report: "over his face and chest."

**I say:** A similar meaning has been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several companions such as Salman, Jabir, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, Anas ibn Malik, and Ibn Abi Mughith from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم in eight narrations. In all of them, the raising of the hands in supplication is mentioned. Thus, there is no sense in the denial by some of raising the hands in supplication, justifying it by saying it is a form of anthropomorphism (تجسيم) because raising the hands toward the sky implies that He, the Exalted, is in it—exalted and sanctified is He above that.

This is a false claim. For the reality of all physical acts of worship is the bringing down of the heart's meaning and the inner orientation to the realm of form, and the manifestation of realities transcendent above matter in the mold of embodiment, as is apparent in prayer (الصلوة), fasting (الصوم), pilgrimage (الحج), and other acts, their parts, and their conditions. Were it not for this, the matter of physical worship would not be established. Among these is supplication, which is the representation of the heart's orientation and the inner asking in the likeness of the asking we are familiar with among ourselves—the asking of the lowly, miserable poor person from the exalted, mighty rich person, where he raises his hands in spreading them and asks for his need with humility and entreaty. Al-Shaykh has narrated in *al-Majalis wa al-Akhbar* with a chain of transmission from Muhammad and Zayd, the sons of ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn, from their father, from their grandfather al-Husayn (peace be upon him), from the Prophet; and in *‘Uddat al-Da‘i* as a *mursal* report, that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم used to raise his hands when he implored and supplicated, just as a pauper begs for food.

In *al-Bihar*, from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), that he heard a man saying: "O Allah, I seek refuge in You from trial (الفتنة)." He (peace be upon him) said: "I see you seeking refuge from your wealth and your children. Allah, the Exalted, says: **أَنَّمَا أَمْوَالُكُمْ وَأَوْلَادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ** (your possessions and your children are only a trial) (8:28). Rather, say: ‘O Allah, I seek refuge in You from the misleading trials (مضلات الفتن).’"

**I say:** This is another door in identifying the meaning of a word, and it has parallels in the narrations. In them, it is stated that the truth in the meaning of every word is what has been mentioned of it in His speech. From this category is what has been mentioned in the narrations regarding the interpretation of the meaning of "the part" (الجزء), "the many" (الكثير), and so on.

In *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed, Allah does not answer a supplication from the back of a heedless heart."

Also in *al-‘Udda*, from ‘Ali (peace be upon him): "Allah does not accept the supplication of a distracted heart."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning. The secret behind it is the lack of realization of the reality of supplication and asking during heedlessness and distraction.

In the *Da‘awat* of al-Rawandi: "In the Torah, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says to the servant: ‘Whenever you continue to call Me against one of My servants because he wronged you, then I have among My servants those who call against you because you wronged them. If you wish, I will answer you and answer him regarding you; and if you wish, I will delay you both until the Day of Resurrection.’"

**I say:** This is because whoever asks for something for himself has accepted it, and by this very acceptance, he has accepted everything similar to it from all aspects. Thus, if he calls against one who wronged him for vengeance, he has called against him because of his wrongdoing; so he is accepting of vengeance against the wrongdoer. If he himself is a wrongdoer to others, he has called against himself with the very thing he called for himself. If he accepts vengeance against himself—and he will never accept it—he is punished with what he wants for others. And if he does not accept that, then supplication has not truly been realized from him. He, the Exalted, said: **وَيَدْعُ الْإِنسَانُ بِالشَّرِّ دُعَاءَهُ بِالْخَيْرِ وَكَانَ الْإِنسَانُ عَجُولًا** — "Man prays for evil as [vehemently as] he prays for good, and man is ever hasty" (17:11).

In *‘Uddat al-Da‘i*: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to Abu Dharr: "O Abu Dharr, shall I not teach you words by which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will benefit you?" I said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "Be mindful of Allah, and Allah will protect you. Be mindful of Allah, and you will find Him before you. Make yourself known to Allah in prosperity, and He will know you in hardship. When you ask, ask Allah; and when you seek help, seek help from Allah. For the Pen has already run with what is to be until the Day of Resurrection; and if all of creation were to strive to benefit you with something that Allah has not written for you, they would not be able to do so."

**I say:** His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Make yourself known to Allah in prosperity, and He will know you in hardship," means: supplicate to Allah in prosperity and do not forget Him, so that He may answer your supplication in hardship and not forget you. This is because whoever forgets his Lord in prosperity has effectively acknowledged the independence of causes in prosperity. Then, when he calls his Lord in hardship, the meaning of his action is that he acknowledges Lordship (الربوبية) in the state of hardship and upon its condition. But He, the Exalted, is not characterized by this; rather, He is the Lord in every state and upon all conditions. Thus, he has not [truly] called his Lord. This meaning has been mentioned in some narrations in another way. In *Makarim al-Akhlaq*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he (peace be upon him) said: "Whoever precedes in supplication is answered when calamity descends, and it is said: ‘A known voice,’ and it is not veiled from the sky. And whoever does not precede in supplication is not answered when calamity descends, and the angels say: ‘We do not know this voice’" (the hadith continues). This is what is understood from the absolute nature of His saying, the Exalted: **نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ** — "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" (9:67). This does not contradict what has been narrated that supplication is not rejected when there is total detachment (الانقطاع), for absolute hardship is different from total detachment.

His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "When you ask, ask Allah; and when you seek help, seek help from Allah," is an instruction to attach oneself to Allah in asking and seeking help in reality. For these conventional causes that are before us—their causality is limited to the limit Allah has ordained for them, not according to their apparent independence in influence. Rather, they possess nothing but the quality of being a path and an intermediary in delivering [the result], while the matter is in the hand of Allah, the Exalted. Therefore, it is mandatory for the servant to turn in his needs toward the Presence of Might and the Door of Grandeur, and not to rely on one cause after another, even though Allah has refused to let matters proceed except through their causes. This is an invitation to not rely on causes except through Allah who bestowed causality upon them; it is not a guidance to negate causes and seek from other than the cause, for that would be a desire for what cannot be desired. How could it be otherwise, when the caller wants what he asks for with the heart, asks for what he wants with the tongue, and seeks help for that with the pillars of his existence—and all of these are causes?

Consider this in the case of a human being, who does what he does with his bodily tools: he gives what he gives with his hand, sees what he sees with his eye, and hears what he hears with his ear. Whoever asks his Lord while negating the causes is like one who asks a person to hand him something without a hand, or to look at him without an eye, or to listen without an ear. And whoever relies on a cause besides Allah, the Glorified, is like one whose heart is attached to the hand of a person in its giving, or to his eye in its seeing, or to his ear in its hearing, while he is heedless and turning away from the person who is the actual agent through those tools. He is thus heedless and deluded. This is not a restriction of the infinite Divine Power, nor a stripping of the Mandatory Choice, just as the limitation we mentioned in the human being does not necessitate stripping him of power and choice; for the limitation in reality returns to the act, not to the agent. For it is necessary that the human is capable of handing, seeing, and hearing, but handing only occurs with the hand, and seeing and hearing are those which occur with the eye and ear, not absolutely. Similarly, the Mandatory One, the Exalted, is capable absolutely, but the specificity of the act depends on the mediation of causes. Zayd, for example—who is an act of Allah—is the human born to so-and-so and so-and-so at such a time and place, and upon the existence of such conditions and the removal of such obstacles. If one of these causes or conditions failed, he would not be himself. Thus, in his creation, he depends on the realization of all of them. The one who is dependent is the act, not the Agent; so understand that.

His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "For the Pen has already run with what is to be until the Day of Resurrection," is a derivation from his saying: "When you ask, ask Allah," in the manner of following the effect with the cause. It is an explanation of the cause and reason for his saying: "When you ask." The meaning is that events are written and ordained by Allah, the Exalted; no cause among the causes has any real influence in them. Therefore, do not ask other than Him, the Exalted, and do not seek help from other than Him, the Exalted. As for Him, the Exalted: His authority is eternal, His kingdom is stable, His will is effective, and every day He is engaged in some work. For this reason, he followed the sentence with his saying: "And if all of creation were to strive..." etc.

Among the reports on supplication is what has been narrated from them extensively: "Supplication is part of the decree (القدر)."

**I say:** In this is the answer to the problem raised by the Jews and others regarding supplication: that the need supplicated for is either already decreed and ordained or it is not. If it is the former, it is necessary; if the latter, it is impossible. In either case, there is no meaning to the influence of supplication. The answer is: the assumption of the decree of a thing's existence does not necessitate its independence from the causes of its existence. Supplication is among the causes of a thing's existence. Thus, with supplication, a cause among the causes of existence is realized, and the effect is realized from its cause. This is the meaning of their saying: "Supplication is part of the decree." There are other narrations with this meaning.

In *al-Bihar*, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Nothing repels the decree (القضاء) except supplication."

From Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Supplication repels the decree after it has been firmly established."

From Abu al-Hasan Musa (peace be upon him): "You must supplicate, for supplication and asking of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, repels calamity. It may have been ordained and decreed such that nothing remains but its execution; then if Allah is called and asked, He diverts the calamity completely."

From Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed, supplication repels the firmly established decree—so increase in supplication, for it is the key to every mercy and the success of every need. What is with Allah is not attained except through supplication, for there is no door that is frequently knocked upon except that it is about to be opened for its seeker."

**I say:** In these is an indication toward persistence (الإصرار), which is among the factors that realize supplication; for the frequent repetition of the intention leads to its purity.

From Isma‘il ibn Hammam, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him): "A servant's secret supplication, a single call, is equal to seventy public calls."

**I say:** In this is an indication toward concealing the supplication and keeping it secret, for it better preserves the sincerity of the request.

In *al-Makarim*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Supplication remains veiled until one sends blessings upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad."

Also from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Whoever puts forward forty of the believers and then supplicates, he will be answered."

Also from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)—when a man from his companions said to him: "I find two verses in the Book of Allah that I seek [the fulfillment of] but do not find it"—he said: "And what are they?" I said: "‘Call Me, and I will hear you,’ yet we call Him and do not see a response." He said: "Do you think Allah broke His promise?" I said: "No." He said: "Then why?" I said: "I do not know." He said: "But I will inform you: whoever obeys Allah in what He commanded him and then calls Him from the direction of supplication (جهة الدعاء), He answers him." I said: "And what is the direction of supplication?" He said: "You begin by praising Allah and glorifying Him, and you mention His blessings upon you and thank Him. Then you send blessings upon Muhammad and his family. Then you mention your sins and acknowledge them, then you seek forgiveness for them. This is the direction of supplication." Then he said: "And what is the other verse?" I said: "‘And whatever you spend, He will replace it’ (34:39), yet I see myself spending and do not see a replacement." He said: "Do you think Allah broke His promise?" I said: "No." He said: "Then why?" I said: "I do not know." He said: "If one of you were to earn wealth from its lawful source and spend it in its rightful place, he would not spend a single dirham except that Allah would replace it for him."

**I say:** The reason for these hadiths regarding the etiquette of supplication is clear, for they bring the servant closer to the reality of supplication and asking.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from Ibn ‘Umar, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "Indeed, when Allah intends to answer a servant, He permits him to supplicate."

Also from Ibn ‘Umar, from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Whoever among you has the door of supplication opened for him, the doors of mercy are opened for him." In another report: "Whoever among you is opened for in supplication, the doors of Paradise are opened for him."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt: "Whoever is given supplication is given the response." Its meaning is clear from what has preceded.

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, from the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "If you knew Allah with the knowledge He is truly entitled to, mountains would move by your supplication."

**I say:** This is because ignorance of the station of Truth and the authority of Lordship, and reliance on causes, necessitates the acknowledgment of real influence for the causes and the restriction of effects to their familiar causes and conventional means. This goes so far that even if a person ceases to acknowledge the real influence of causes, he remains acknowledging the necessity of the paths and the mediation of the intermediary causes. For we see that movement and travel necessitate approaching the destination. Then, if the belief in the real influence of travel in approaching ceases, we believe that travel is an intermediary and Allah, the Glorified, is the Influencer there; yet the belief remains in the necessity of the mediation—that were it not for the travel, there would be no closeness or approaching. In short, that effects do not fail to follow their causes, even if the causes possess nothing but mediation without influence. This is what knowledge of the station of Allah, the Glorified, does not validate, for it does not suit the absolute Divine Authority. This illusion is what necessitated our belief in the impossibility of effects failing to follow their conventional causes—such as weight and attraction from a body, closeness from movement, satiety from eating, quenching from drinking, and so on. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on miracles (الإعجاز) that the law of cause and effect—or in other words, the mediation of causes between Allah, the Glorified, and His effects—is a truth that cannot be avoided. However, it does not necessitate restricting events to their conventional causes. Rather, theoretical rational inquiry, the Book, and the Sunnah establish the principle of mediation and nullify the restriction. Yes, rational impossibilities (المحالات العقلية) are not to be hoped for.

Once you know this, you will know that knowledge of Allah necessitates the acknowledgment that whatever is not an essential impossibility (محال ذاتي) among all that habit deems impossible—supplication is answered regarding it, just as the core of the miracles of the Prophets returns to the answering of supplication.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "So let them respond to Me, and let them believe in Me": "They should know that I am capable of giving them what they ask of Me."

In *al-Majma‘*, he said: It is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "‘And let them believe in Me,’ meaning let them be certain that I am capable of giving them what they asked for. ‘So that they may be rightly guided,’ meaning so that they may hit upon the truth, i.e., be guided to it."
